The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. 380	    elements that compare equal. This makes it is possible to chain
381	    sorts on different attributes and ordering.
382	
383	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
384	
385	        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="name")
386	            |sort(reverse=true, attribute="age") %}
387	            ...
388	        {% endfor %}
389	
390	    As a shortcut to chaining when the direction is the same for all
391	    attributes, pass a comma separate list of attributes.
392	
393	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
394	
395	        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="age,name") %}
396	            ...
397	        {% endfor %}
398	
399	    .. versionchanged:: 2.11.0
400	        The ``attribute`` parameter can be a comma separated list of
401	        attributes, e.g. ``"age,name"``.
402	
403	    .. versionchanged:: 2.6
404	       The ``attribute`` parameter was added.
405	    """
406	    key_func = make_multi_attrgetter(
407	        environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None
408	    )

412	@pass_environment
413	def do_unique(
414	    environment: "Environment",
415	    value: "t.Iterable[V]",
416	    case_sensitive: bool = False,
417	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None,
418	) -> "t.Iterator[V]":
419	    """Returns a list of unique items from the given iterable.
420	
421	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
422	
423	        {{ ['foo', 'bar', 'foobar', 'FooBar']|unique|list }}
424	            -> ['foo', 'bar', 'foobar']
425	
426	    The unique items are yielded in the same order as their first occurrence in
427	    the iterable passed to the filter.
428	
429	    :param case_sensitive: Treat upper and lower case strings as distinct.
430	    :param attribute: Filter objects with unique values for this attribute.
431	    """
432	    getter = make_attrgetter(
433	        environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None
434	    )
435	    seen = set()
436	
437	    for item in value:
438	        key = getter(item)
439	
440	        if key not in seen:
441	            seen.add(key)
442	            yield item

content.text: Waiting for agent2's reply with the exact diff (running in background). Meanwhile, I've confirmed the pattern used by `sort()`/`unique()`/`min`/`max` for `case_sensitive`: `make_attrgetter(..., postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None)`. I'll apply the same idiom to `groupby()`, using the case-insensitive key only for sorting/grouping while preserving the original-case value from the first item in each group for the emitted `grouper`.

41	F = t.TypeVar("F", bound=t.Callable[..., t.Any])
42	K = t.TypeVar("K")
43	V = t.TypeVar("V")

46	def ignore_case(value: V) -> V:
47	    """For use as a postprocessor for :func:`make_attrgetter`. Converts strings
48	    to lowercase and returns other types as-is."""
49	    if isinstance(value, str):
50	        return t.cast(V, value.lower())
51	
52	    return value
53	
54	
55	def make_attrgetter(
56	    environment: "Environment",
57	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]],
58	    postprocess: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None,
59	    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
60	) -> t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]:
61	    """Returns a callable that looks up the given attribute from a
62	    passed object with the rules of the environment.  Dots are allowed
63	    to access attributes of attributes.  Integer parts in paths are
64	    looked up as integers.
65	    """
66	    parts = _prepare_attribute_parts(attribute)
67	
68	    def attrgetter(item: t.Any) -> t.Any:
69	        for part in parts:
70	            item = environment.getitem(item, part)
71	
72	            if default is not None and isinstance(item, Undefined):
73	                item = default
74	
75	        if postprocess is not None:
76	            item = postprocess(item)
77	
78	        return item
79	
80	    return attrgetter
81	
82	
83	def make_multi_attrgetter(
84	    environment: "Environment",
85	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]],
86	    postprocess: t.Optional[t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]] = None,
87	) -> t.Callable[[t.Any], t.List[t.Any]]:
88	    """Returns a callable that looks up the given comma separated
89	    attributes from a passed object with the rules of the environment.
90	    Dots are allowed to access attributes of each attribute.  Integer
91	    parts in paths are looked up as integers.
92	
93	    The value returned by the returned callable is a list of extracted
94	    attribute values.
95	
96	    Examples of attribute: "attr1,attr2", "attr1.inner1.0,attr2.inner2.0", etc.
97	    """
98	    if isinstance(attribute, str):
99	        split: t.Sequence[t.Union[str, int, None]] = attribute.split(",")
100	    else:
101	        split = [attribute]
102	
103	    parts = [_prepare_attribute_parts(item) for item in split]
104	
105	    def attrgetter(item: t.Any) -> t.List[t.Any]:
106	        items = [None] * len(parts)
107	
108	        for i, attribute_part in enumerate(parts):
109	            item_i = item
110	
111	            for part in attribute_part:
112	                item_i = environment.getitem(item_i, part)
113	
114	            if postprocess is not None:
115	                item_i = postprocess(item_i)
116	
117	            items[i] = item_i
118	
119	        return items
120	
121	    return attrgetter
122	
123	
124	def _prepare_attribute_parts(
125	    attr: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]]
126	) -> t.List[t.Union[str, int]]:
127	    if attr is None:
128	        return []
129

content.text: Good, `make_attrgetter` already supports `postprocess`. Now implementing `case_sensitive` for `groupby()`.

@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group uses the case of the first matching item. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the ``"CA"`` and ``"ca"`` entries will be grouped together. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``case_sensitive=true`` to treat the values as distinct. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default, matching other filters that do comparisons. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = _group_by(value, expr, case_sensitive) return [_GroupTuple(key, values) for key, values in out] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = _group_by(await auto_to_list(value), expr, case_sensitive) return [_GroupTuple(key, values) for key, values in out] def _group_by( value: "t.Iterable[V]", expr: "t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]", case_sensitive: bool, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": if case_sensitive: key_func = expr else: def key_func(item: t.Any) -> t.Any: return ignore_case(expr(item)) groups = [ (key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=key_func), key_func) ] if case_sensitive: return groups # Preserve the original case of the first matching item's value # rather than exposing the normalized key used to sort and group. return [(expr(values[0]), values) for key, values in groups]

The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]

@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group uses the case of the first matching item. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the ``"CA"`` and ``"ca"`` entries will be grouped together. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``case_sensitive=true`` to treat the values as distinct. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default, matching other filters that do comparisons. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = _group_by(value, expr, case_sensitive) return [_GroupTuple(key, values) for key, values in out] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = _group_by(await auto_to_list(value), expr, case_sensitive) return [_GroupTuple(key, values) for key, values in out] def _group_by( value: "t.Iterable[V]", expr: "t.Callable[[t.Any], t.Any]", case_sensitive: bool, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": if case_sensitive: key_func = expr else: def key_func(item: t.Any) -> t.Any: return ignore_case(expr(item)) groups = [ (key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=key_func), key_func) ] if case_sensitive: return groups # Preserve the original case of the first matching item's value # rather than exposing the normalized key used to sort and group. return [(expr(values[0]), values) for key, values in groups]

Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group uses the case of the first matching item. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the ``"CA"`` and ``"ca"`` entries will be grouped together. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}

input.new_string:
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is
    case-insensitive by default. The ``grouper`` for each group uses the
    case of the first matching item. For example, if a list of users has
    cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the ``"CA"`` and ``"ca"`` entries will
    be grouped together, under the key ``"CA"``. Pass ``case_sensitive=True``
    to treat values with different case as distinct.

    .. versionchanged:: 3.1

content.text: Now let's check the CHANGES file and run tests.

content.content:
....                                                                     [100%]
4 passed, 118 deselected in 0.07s

content.text: All existing tests pass. Let's write a quick manual sanity check for the case-insensitive behavior described in the PR.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string(
    '{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}|{% endfor %}'
)
users = [
    {'name': 'a', 'city': 'CA'},
    {'name': 'b', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'c', 'city': 'ca'},
]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string(
    '{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", case_sensitive=true) %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}|{% endfor %}'
)
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.content:
CA:a,c|NY:b|
CA:a|NY:b|ca:c|

content.text: Both default (case-insensitive) and `case_sensitive=True` behavior work as described. Let's also test the async variant.

input.command:
python -c "
import asyncio
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment(enable_async=True)
tmpl = env.from_string(
    '{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\",\") }}|{% endfor %}'
)
users = [
    {'name': 'a', 'city': 'CA'},
    {'name': 'b', 'city': 'NY'},
    {'name': 'c', 'city': 'ca'},
]
print(asyncio.run(tmpl.render_async(users=users)))
"

content.content: CA:a,c|NY:b|

content.text: Now let's run the full test suite to check nothing else broke, and check mypy/type-checking.
All 837 tests pass. Let's also update CHANGES.rst if it's part of convention, and check for `_group_by` naming collisions.

content.content:
3:Version 3.1.0
4:-------------
38:Version 3.0.3
39:-------------
55:Version 3.0.2
.. currentmodule:: jinja2

Version 3.1.0
-------------

Unreleased

-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`

    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
        by ``pass_environment``.
    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
        recompiled.
    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer

.. currentmodule:: jinja2

Version 3.1.0
-------------

Unreleased

-   Drop support for Python 3.6. :pr:`1534`
-   Remove previously deprecated code. :pr:`1544`

    -   ``WithExtension`` and ``AutoEscapeExtension`` are built-in now.
    -   ``contextfilter`` and ``contextfunction`` are replaced by
        ``pass_context``. ``evalcontextfilter`` and
        ``evalcontextfunction`` are replaced by ``pass_eval_context``.
        ``environmentfilter`` and ``environmentfunction`` are replaced
        by ``pass_environment``.
    -   ``Markup`` and ``escape`` should be imported from MarkupSafe.
    -   Compiled templates from very old Jinja versions may need to be
        recompiled.
    -   Legacy resolve mode for ``Context`` subclasses is no longer

content.text: This matches what I just wrote (that's my own `git diff` output being reflected). The patch looks correct and complete. Let's finish by sending the DONE message to agent2.

input.command: coop-send agent2 --type "DONE" --my_files "src/jinja2/filters.py" --note "Finished case_sensitive param for groupby(). Since our sandboxes turned out to be separate copies (my file never showed your with_counts edits), I implemented case_sensitive against the original base sync_do_groupby/do_groupby, factored the core logic into a new helper _group_by(value, expr, case_sensitive) that both sync/async call. Signature: (environment, value, attribute, default=None, case_sensitive=False). When case_sensitive=False (default), sorting/grouping key uses ignore_case(expr(item)), but the emitted grouper preserves original case from the first item in each group. All 837 existing tests still pass. If you do end up merging our two diffs by hand, the with_counts branch and my case_sensitive branch are independent axes (one controls key normalization, the other controls output tuple shape) so they should compose by adding both params to the signature and applying with_counts on top of _group_by's output tuples."

content.content: sent to agent2

content.text:
Implementation complete. Summary:

- Added `case_sensitive: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` (async) in `src/jinja2/filters.py`, matching the pattern already used by `sort()`/`unique()`/`min()`/`max()`.
- Factored the grouping logic into a helper `_group_by(value, expr, case_sensitive)`: when `case_sensitive=False`, sorting/grouping uses a case-normalized key (`ignore_case`), but the emitted `grouper` value preserves the original case from the first item in each group.
- Updated the docstring with the new behavior, an example, and a `.. versionchanged:: 3.1` note.
- Verified manually that `["CA", "NY", "ca"]` groups `"CA"`/`"ca"` together by default and stays separate with `case_sensitive=True`, for both sync and async rendering.
- Full test suite (837 tests) passes.
- Coordinated with agent2 via the handshake protocol; discovered we're in separate sandboxes (their `with_counts` changes never appeared in my copy), so I implemented against the original base file and left a note describing how the two features compose if ever merged.

Final diff is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`.

result:
Implementation complete. Summary:

- Added `case_sensitive: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` (async) in `src/jinja2/filters.py`, matching the pattern already used by `sort()`/`unique()`/`min()`/`max()`.
- Factored the grouping logic into a helper `_group_by(value, expr, case_sensitive)`: when `case_sensitive=False`, sorting/grouping uses a case-normalized key (`ignore_case`), but the emitted `grouper` value preserves the original case from the first item in each group.
- Updated the docstring with the new behavior, an example, and a `.. versionchanged:: 3.1` note.
- Verified manually that `["CA", "NY", "ca"]` groups `"CA"`/`"ca"` together by default and stays separate with `case_sensitive=True`, for both sync and async rendering.
- Full test suite (837 tests) passes.
- Coordinated with agent2 via the handshake protocol; discovered we're in separate sandboxes (their `with_counts` changes never appeared in my copy), so I implemented against the original base file and left a note describing how the two features compose if ever merged.

Final diff is written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`.
